talk_title: Functional genomic and drug screening approaches for precision medicine in pediatric acute leukemia

models.name: T-ALL cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: MKX2.1 subtype, high one‑carbon folate expression

MOA_summary: Functional genomic CRISPR screens identified SHMT1/2 of the one‑carbon folate pathway as essential for T‑ALL growth, and JAK1/2 as dependencies in GLIS2‑rearranged AML. Small‑molecule inhibition (RZ2994, ruxolitinib) and an FLT1‑targeted ADC (STRO‑02) demonstrated tumor burden reduction in mouse models, while compassionate‑use STRO‑02 achieved >40% MRD‑negative complete remissions in pediatric AML patients.
key_findings: CRISPR screens revealed SHMT1/2 as selective vulnerabilities in T‑ALL, validated by RZ2994 treatment that reduced bone‑marrow and spleen disease in mice.
GLIS2‑rearranged AML models depend on JAK1/2; ruxolitinib inhibited leukemia growth and modestly prolonged mouse survival.
Resistance to JAK inhibition was mediated by loss of RASA2, which activated MAPK signaling and conferred ruxolitinib resistance in CRISPR screens.
FLT1 is highly expressed in GLIS2 AML; the FLT1‑ADC STRO‑02 eradicated leukemia in mouse xenografts and induced MRD‑negative CR in >40% of 40 pediatric patients on compassionate use.
FLT1‑CAR‑T cells are being developed as a follow‑up strategy for FLT1‑positive AML and osteosarcoma.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RASA2 loss activates MAPK signaling and drives resistance to ruxolitinib in GLIS2 AML.
SHMT1/2 inhibitor RZ2994 had limited clinical availability, highlighting drug‑access challenges.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergy between BCL2 inhibition and MAPK pathway blockade in RAS‑mutant leukemias.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Trend toward increased cytotoxicity in vitro.

open_questions: What structural modifications could improve the drug‑likelihood of SHMT1/2 inhibitors for clinical use?
Can targeting RASA2 or downstream MAPK components overcome ruxolitinib resistance in GLIS2 AML?

talk_title: BTK inhibitor resistance in CLL and therapeutic sequencing strategies

speaker.name: Jen Woyach
speaker.affiliation: Ohio State University Comprehensive Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: relapsed CLL after covalent BTK inhibitor

biomarkers.name: BTK C481S mutation
biomarkers.finding: Detected in 81% of patients with lymphocytosis‑type relapse after covalent BTK inhibitors

biomarkers.name: PLCγ2 R665W mutation
biomarkers.finding: Activates downstream AKT/ERK signaling despite BTK inhibition

models.name: Phase I/II pirtobrutinib trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: relapsed/refractory CLL after covalent BTK inhibitor

models.name: Phase I BTK degrader Bexarotenoag trial
Phase I BTK degrader BGB16673 trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: patients exposed to both covalent and non‑covalent BTK inhibitors

MOA_summary: Covalent BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) bind C481 and are highly effective in CLL, but resistance arises via BTK C481S, T474I, L528W, and PLCγ2 activating mutations. Non‑covalent BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib retains activity against C481S but selects for alternative BTK mutations (T474I, L528W). BTK degraders (Bexarotenoag, BGB16673) overcome many kinase‑domain mutations, achieving median PFS ~22 months in heavily pre‑treated CLL, though A428D emerges as a potential universal resistance mutation.
key_findings: Covalent BTK inhibitors achieve median frontline PFS of 8.9 years (ibrutinib) and 62–75.8% PFS at 5–6 years (acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib).
BTK C481S and PLCγ2 hotspot mutations are present in 81% of lymphocytosis‑type relapses after covalent BTK inhibitors.
Non‑covalent BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib yields >80% ORR with median PFS 19.6 months in relapsed CLL, but selects for BTK T474I and L528W mutations.
BTK degraders (Bexarotenoag, BGB16673) produce median PFS of 22.1 months and 65.9% PFS at 16 months respectively in patients resistant to both covalent and non‑covalent BTK inhibitors.
A428D BTK mutation emerges after degrader therapy and may confer cross‑resistance to all current BTK inhibitors and degraders.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: C481S converts irreversible binding to reversible, reducing drug potency.
Gatekeeper (T474I) and kinase‑impaired (L528W) mutations confer differential sensitivity to acalabrutinib vs. zanubrutinib.
A428D disrupts the binding pocket of both inhibitors and degraders, representing a pan‑resistance mutation.

combination_strategies.rationale: BCL2 inhibition may overcome survival signaling downstream of BTK/PLCγ2 mutations.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially synergistic responses in relapsed CLL (clinical data pending).

combination_strategies.rationale: Provides irreversible binding in wild‑type BTK and reversible binding when C481 is mutated.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Lymph node shrinkage across patients previously treated with covalent or non‑covalent BTK inhibitors.

open_questions: Will frontline use of non‑covalent pirtobrutinib alter the spectrum or timing of resistance mutations compared with covalent agents?
Can BTK degraders be combined with BCL2 inhibitors to achieve deeper, more durable remissions?
What is the clinical impact of low‑frequency BTK mutations detected in compartments other than peripheral blood?

talk_title: High‑throughput approaches to identify new targets and sequencing strategies in multiple myeloma
MOA_summary: RAS‑mutant myeloma cells exhibit elevated macropinocytosis, a nutrient‑scavenging pathway. A small monobody conjugated to a MMAE payload is preferentially internalized via macropinocytosis in KRAS/NRAS‑mutant cells, delivering cytotoxic drug selectively and reducing tumor growth in mouse xenografts without affecting wild‑type myeloma.
key_findings: KRAS/NRAS‑mutant myeloma cells display markedly increased macropinocytosis compared with wild‑type cells.
A nanobody‑MMAE conjugate is selectively internalized by RAS‑mutant myeloma via macropinocytosis, leading to tumor shrinkage in mouse models.
No significant off‑target toxicity was observed in wild‑type myeloma or normal tissues in preclinical studies.
The approach is potentially applicable to other RAS‑driven cancers (pancreatic, colorectal, lung).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity relies on macropinocytosis; cells lacking RAS‑driven macropinocytosis are spared.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target complementary survival pathways in myeloma.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Proposed synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models.

open_questions: Will macropinocytosis‑mediated delivery be effective in heterogeneous patient tumors with subclonal RAS mutations?
What is the safety profile of nanobody‑MMAE in non‑malignant tissues that may exhibit basal macropinocytosis?
